citations.narrative:
In the enclosure housing two goats and two pigs, there is an electric cord for the heating lamp draped across the
enclosure wall where the animals can access it. There are also multiple strands of plastic baling twine in the enclosure
where the animals can access them. The facility removed these items during the inspection.
Access to inappropriate materials, such as baling twine or electric cords, can lead to illness, injury, or entanglement. All
housing facilities must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. All
housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and
to contain the animals.
To be corrected by April 7, 2026.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
Housekeeping in building A needs to be addressed. Along one wall, multiple enclosures are kept next to a shelf storing a
random assortment of items. One of the enclosures was pushed against an area of the shelf that was storing fabric, an
unplugged electric cord, and other items. The macaw hybrid in this enclosure was able to access these items through the
bars and had pulled some of the fabric into the enclosure. Another enclosure had pieces of plastic tape stuck on the bars
where the Congo African Grey inside could access it.
Improper storage and accumulation of items in a housing facility can allow birds to gain access to inappropriate items that
could cause illness or injury. Premises where housing facilities are located, including buildings, surrounding grounds, and
exhibit areas, must be kept clean and in good repair in order to protect the birds from injury and disease, to facilitate the
husbandry practices required in this subpart, and to reduce or eliminate breeding and living areas for rodents, pests, and
vermin. Premises must be kept free of accumulations of trash, junk, waste products, and discarded matter. Weeds,
grasses, and bushes must be controlled so as to facilitate cleaning of the premises and pest control, and to protect the
health and well-being of the birds.
To be corrected by April 7, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
In the enclosure housing two goats and two pigs, there is an electric cord for the heating lamp draped across the 
enclosure wall where the animals can access it. There are also multiple strands of plastic baling twine in the enclosure 
where the animals can access them. The facility removed these items during the inspection. 
Access to inappropriate materials, such as baling twine or electric cords, can lead to illness, injury, or entanglement. All 
housing facilities must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. All 
housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and 
to contain the animals. 
To be corrected by April 7, 2026. 
3.158(c) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
Housekeeping in building A needs to be addressed. Along one wall, multiple enclosures are kept next to a shelf storing a 
random assortment of items. One of the enclosures was pushed against an area of the shelf that was storing fabric, an 
unplugged electric cord, and other items. The macaw hybrid in this enclosure was able to access these items through the 
bars and had pulled some of the fabric into the enclosure. Another enclosure had pieces of plastic tape stuck on the bars 
where the Congo African Grey inside could access it.  
Improper storage and accumulation of items in a housing facility can allow birds to gain access to inappropriate items that 
could cause illness or injury. Premises where housing facilities are located, including buildings, surrounding grounds, and 
exhibit areas, must be kept clean and in good repair in order to protect the birds from injury and disease, to facilitate the 
husbandry practices required in this subpart, and to reduce or eliminate breeding and living areas for rodents, pests, and 
vermin. Premises must be kept free of accumulations of trash, junk, waste products, and discarded matter. Weeds, 
grasses, and bushes must be controlled so as to facilitate cleaning of the premises and pest control, and to protect the 
health and well-being of the birds. 
To be corrected by April 7, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
